title: Meat preparation for meat identification
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9os5q","text":"Identifying species by identifying unique DNA sequences is common practice. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dsu9i","text":"In this case we will identify the presence or absence of pork DNA in a meat sample.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4pbar","text":"We will use the alkaline method for DNA extraction and LAMP method for DNA amplification and detection.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bjpa8","text":"This is not a new protocol but rather the detailing of a previously published protocol listed in the manuscript citation.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"64jag","text":"Pork meat identification protocol. Meat Preparation protocol based on","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9sfee","text":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3550954/","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":53}],"data":{}},{"key":"f51bb","text":"\"About 500 mg of meat was triturated in pestle \u0026 mortar with four volumes (4 ml) of 0.2N NaOH. 5 μl of the extract was again mixed with eight volumes (40 μl) of 0.2N NaOH in a sterile micro centrifuge tube. Mix was heated at 75 °C in water bath for 20 min and then added eight volumes (360 μl) of 0.04 M Tris HCl (pH 7.75) for neutralization of pH. 1 μl of final mix containing about 100 ng DNA was used for.\"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"40133E177B5011EFA8380A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3550954"}}}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"1boqi","text":"Use appropriate PPE and follow Safe Work Practices.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"62oid","text":"NaOH (sodium hydroxide or caustic soda) needs to be handled with gloves because it's a highly corrosive alkali.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ce978","text":"Seek and follow Safety Data Sheets for NaOH and HCl","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"stnr","text":"Consumables: 15 ml falcon tube, 1.5 ml centrifuge tube or equivalent, 1 PCR tube 0.1 mL PCR tubes with flat caps, commonly known as low-profile PCR tubes that matches the device well size.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b281n","text":"Labware:  graduated cylinder","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"avi6r","text":"Devices: Mortar and pestle, LAMP/PCR/Water bath and thermometer, scale, 1- 10 μl pipette, 100- 1000 μl pipette and tips. Optional: vortex, LAMP or PCR device or water bath, scale and boat.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ed3u4","text":"Reagents: NaOH, Tris, HCl, DNase free water, meat sample to be tested","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"er5ht","text":"Others: stir rod, spatula, nitrile gloves, pipette rack , tube rack, labeling pen,  tube rack.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Prepare 10 ml of NaOH 0.2 mol/L (sodium hydroxide or caustic soda). This will be used twice, once for 4 mL and once for 40μL.
Aliquot 5 μL from the mix and add it to a new sterile PCR (100 μl) tube labeled "prepared sample" . This is done with a 1- 10 μl pipette, preferably using filtered tips.
Add 40μL from the tube labelled "NaOH 0.2 mol/L" to the "prepared sample" tube .
Heat the resulting mix (45 μL) at 75 Celsius for 20 minutes in either water bath, LAMP or PCR device.

Move the content of the "prepared sample" to a new 1.5 ml centrifuge tube labeled final prepared sample and add 360 μl of 0.04 mol/L Tris HCl (pH 7.75) prepared using the https://www.protocols.io/view/prepare-tris-buffer-for-meat-identification-protoc-dndu5a6w protocol. !!! Check for the latest version. This is done for for neutralization of pH. Note and warning. The paper specifies pH=7 while the referenced paper specifies pH=7.75 Note: We assume pH is measured at 25 Celsius. Store at -20 .

Label a new 15 ml falcon tube "NaOH 0.2 mol/L" and add 10 mL DNAfree water and 0.080 g of NaOH. Mix to dissolve, manually or using a vortex. For general molarity calculations see Molarity calculation . Molar concentration is the number of moles contained in one Liter of a substance. The concentration of a solution is expressed in mol/L C=(m/M)/V where V is the volume in liters, m is mass in grams and M is molar weight? in grams/mol https://www.alloprof.qc.ca/en/students/vl/sciences/calculating-molar-concentration-s1622 So: Molecular Weight of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) =40 g/mol: Na=22.989g/mol O=15.999g/mol H=1.008g/mol ( http://chembook.org/page-nonav.php?chnum=1§=5) so: 22.989g/mol+15.999g/mol+1.008g/mol=39.996g/mol so about 40 g/mol. so m=C*M*V =0.2mol/L*40g/mol *10 mL= 8 g/L*10 mL=80 mg of NaOH
Optional, before freezing at -20, aliquot 1 μl measure the result using a NanoDrop or equivalent device.That should normally contain about 100 ng of DNA according to the reference paper.
Add 0.5 g fresh pork meat without fat in a pestle and mortar and add 4 ml NaOH from the tube labelled "NaOH 0.2 mol/L".
Optionally filter the result from previous step using a syringe and Luer filter.
triturate (grind ) the mix . This will break the structure and results in a paste/liquid.
creator: openbioscience Adrian
